item.title: Few-shot Image Registration of brain MRI using Meta Learning
item.description:
Medical image registration is a crucial task in medical image analysis and has several applications, such as surgical planning, disease diagnosis, and treatment monitoring. Traditional image registration approaches require large amounts of annotated data, making them computationally expensive and time-consuming. However, with the emergence of few-shot learning, researchers have been exploring novel ways to perform image registration with limited annotated data.

Our project aims to develop a modified synthmorph model with the iMAML (Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning with implicit gradients)[1] approach and weighted training for few-shot learning. The project’s goal is to register brain MRIs with limited annotated data using a few-shot learning approach. The synthmorph model is a deep generative model that learns to map images from one domain to another using a small number of training examples. The model’s objective is to generate new images in the target domain that are similar to the input images in the source domain.

We are modifying the synthmorph model to incorporate the iMAML approach to enable the model to adapt to new tasks with few-shot examples quickly. We are also incorporating weighted training to the modified model to improve its performance on rare or difficult cases.

Our approach involves training the modified synthmorph model brain MRIs datasets, where we randomly select a small number of images from the dataset for training, evaluating the model’s performance on a test set and compare its results with traditional image registration approaches. We are using PyTorch to implement the model and evaluate its performance.

Our preliminary results are promising in registering brain MRIs using few-shot learning. We believe that our approach can significantly improve medical image registration and pave the way for new applications in medical image analysis.

In conclusion, the proposed project has the potential to advance the field of medical image analysis and provide valuable tools for healthcare professionals to improve patient diagnosis and treatment, potentiallly revolutionizing medical image registration and make it more efficient and accessible.

———–

[1] Rajeswaran, Aravind, et al. “Meta-learning with implicit gradients.” Advances in neural information processing systems 32 (2019).

item.title: CapsuleGAN - Improving GANs with CapsuleNET
item.description:
Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) have shown remarkable success in generating realistic images, but they still suffer from some limitations such as mode collapse and instability during training. One approach to addressing these issues is to use Capsule Networks, a deep learning architecture that can better handle hierarchical representations and spatial relationships.

Capsule Networks were introduced by Hinton et al. in 2017 as an alternative to convolutional neural networks (CNNs). They are composed of capsules, which are groups of neurons that encode the presence and pose of an object in an image. Capsule Networks are designed to better handle hierarchical representations by modeling the parts of an object and their spatial relationships.

Our project implements the Capsule Network architecture in the discriminator of a GAN model and incorporates the Capsule Network margin loss into the objective function. Our results show that CapsuleGAN generates more diverse and realistic images than traditional GAN models. We also found that CapsuleGAN is more stable during training as evidenced by the lower variation in the generator and discriminator losses. We used the CIFAR-10 dataset to train and evaluate our model.

The Capsule Network architecture was particularly effective in capturing spatial relationships between objects, leading to more realistic and coherent images. Capsule Networks can better handle hierarchical representations and spatial relationships, which are important for generating high-quality images. Incorporating the margin loss of Capsule Networks can also improve the stability of GANs during training.

In conclusion, our project demonstrates the potential of Capsule Networks in enhancing the performance of GAN models and advancing the field of generative deep learning. Future research could explore further improvements by incorporating Capsule Networks into other types of GAN models or applying them to different domains beyond image generation.

item.title: Early Fault Detection of Turbo Engines using Time Series Analysis
item.description: Developing a method for predicting the remaining useful life (RUL) of a dynamic system is essential to deploy timely maintenance operations and extending the life of service. In this project, we propose an LSTM based Time Series Analysis method for early fault detection and alert system. As a first step, we will use the NASA Turbo Engines dataset for training our algorithm. The method will use historical data from 26 sensors to predict the future state (RUL) of the Turbo Engine. Further, we would like to extend this model into the real world predictive maintenance task. The project has the potential to improve the safety and reliability of any dynamic system performing under stress (e.g. aircraft operations) by providing early detection of faults.

item.title: Electrical Substation Detection from Satellite Data
item.description:
Introduction

Electrical substations are critical components of the power grid, responsible for converting high voltage electricity into lower voltages that can be used by homes and businesses. However, locating and identifying these substations can be challenging, especially in large areas with complex terrain. This is where satellite imagery and deep learning can come in handy. In this project, we explore how to use these technologies to detect electrical substations from satellite images.

Data Collection and Preprocessing

To train our deep learning model, we needed a dataset of satellite images labeled with the locations of electrical substations. We obtained such a dataset from the Google Earth Engine API (stored already in a dataset link), which combines a wide range of satellite imagery and geospatial data. The dataset included high-resolution images of various regions around the world, with labels indicating the locations of electrical substations.

To preprocess the data, we first resized the images to a smaller resolution to reduce computational requirements. We also normalized the pixel values of the images to a range of 0 to 1. To further improve the performance of our model, we applied extensive data augmentation techniques, including random rotations, translations, and zooms. This helped our model learn to detect electrical substations under a variety of conditions and perspectives.

Model Architecture and Training

For our deep learning model, we chose the U-Net architecture, which is commonly used in image segmentation tasks. U-Net consists of a contracting path, which captures the context and spatial information of the input image, and an expanding path, which uses this information to generate a segmentation mask of the image. We modified the U-Net architecture to output a binary mask indicating the presence or absence of an electrical substation in the input image.

We trained our model using TensorFlow, a popular deep learning library. We used a combination of binary cross-entropy loss and dice loss as the training objective, which helped our model learn to accurately localize electrical substations in the input images. We also used an Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.0001 to train the model.

Results and Future Work

Our trained model achieved an accuracy of 95% in localizing electrical substations in the test dataset. This is a promising result, considering the complexity and variability of the input images. However, there is still room for improvement, especially in regions with dense vegetation or complex terrain.

In the future, we plan to explore other deep learning architectures and data augmentation techniques to improve the performance of our model. We also plan to test our model on other datasets and compare its performance with other state-of-the-art methods for detecting electrical substations from satellite images. Overall, we believe that our project has the potential to help power grid operators and researchers locate and monitor electrical substations more effectively and efficiently.

The code can be found here
